body_txt: A character whose motivations and overall personality essentially revolve around their interaction with another, possibly more interesting, character with whom they really should be on an equal standing. Without this interaction, they would otherwise be pretty bland. Sometimes a series will remove that "central" person from the equation somehow, and the Satellite Character has to establish their own motives. This is a common way to make a Battle Butler more interesting. On the other hand, a Spin-Off can fail entirely if the star is a Satellite Character whose character can't support their own stories. Contributing to their poor image in some circles, badly-written Magical Girlfriends become Satellite Characters if not outright Satellite Love Interests with disturbing frequency. Note that not all satellite characters are friendly. Some satellites are the exact opposite, full of all-consuming jealousy, rage, bitterness, vengefulness, or outright hatred, orbiting until the time is right to crash violently into the character they circle. This is less common, though. It's worth noting, however, that this trope can't always be avoided. Realistically, not every character in a story is going to be fully fleshed-out and their interactions with others will often be dependent on what specific role they serve. This is especially true in the case of minor characters, who otherwise wouldn't be given much screentime to begin with, as well as characters involved in other characters' backstories who are dead or otherwise uninvolved in the present story. This goes double for more plot-oriented works, where the focus leans towards the actual narrative as opposed to character depth. Likely to count if the character is the Token Black Friend , Gay Best Friend , Hollywood Homely , Uncool Redhead , or even the character's own parent. Contrast The Friends Who Never Hang , where a character has developed interaction with a much larger number of characters except for one or two significant exceptions. Similar to Flat Character , in that their characterization/role in the story revolves around one thing, in this case being another character. The romantic-only variant of this trope is Satellite Love Interest and familial-only variant is Satellite Family Member . Has nothing to do with a story where the character in question is a living Satellite, nor a character that happened to be punched or rocketed into orbit.
